title: Help Defense: See the Ball, See Your Man

lesson.title: How to be in two places at once
lesson.body_md:
## The hardest part of defense isn't your man

Guarding your man is reactive — they move, you move. Help defense is **predictive**. You have to see a play developing before it happens and be in the right spot before the ball gets there.

## The golden rule

> *"See the ball, see your man."*

Your eyes pivot constantly. Never stare at one. Never lose the other.

## Where to stand: the help line

Imagine a line straight from the ball to the rim. You should be **off** your man, sitting somewhere between your man and that line. The farther your man is from the ball, the deeper into help you sit.

- **Strong-side help (your man near the ball):** one arm's length off, ready to dig.
- **Weak-side help (your man across the floor):** way off — almost in the paint — ready to rotate.

## When to help

Help comes in two flavors:

### 1. Stunt and recover
Ball is driving past your teammate. You take **one hard step** at the ball with your hands out, then sprint back to your man. Buys your teammate a second to recover.

### 2. Full rotate
Ball beats your teammate clean. You **commit** to the ball, take the charge or contest the layup. Now your teammate has to rotate to your man.

## How to know which one

- Teammate just got a step beat? **Stunt.**
- Teammate is gone, layup is coming? **Rotate.**
- Drive coming at you with no one behind? **Hold the line.** Don't gamble.

## The 3 mistakes that wreck help

1. **Ball watching.** You stare at the ball, your man cuts behind you for a layup.
2. **Helping too early.** You leave on a fake, the ball gets swung, your man buries a three.
3. **Not helping at all.** You stay glued to your man and your teammate gets cooked. Defense is a **team** activity.

## The communication piece

Help defense breaks without talk. Three calls every defender needs:

- **"Ball!"** — I see it, I'm guarding it.
- **"Help!"** — I've got your back if you get beat.
- **"Switch!"** — We're swapping assignments, now.

Loud, early, and constant. Silent help is bad help.

## What you're training

This module's scenarios show you a drive starting and ask: stunt, rotate, or hold. The wrong choice gives up either an open three or a layup. The right choice keeps the defense connected.

## Coach's cue

> *"Be one pass away with your eyes, two passes away with your feet."*

Good help defenders look like they're cheating. They're not — they're just reading the next play before it happens.
